ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn URI exists specifically for Double Ka Meetha. The closest defensible FoodOn class is 'bread pudding food product' (FOODON_03315091), which captures the bread-soaked-in-milk-and-sugar preparation method.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (Bread pudding) is the closest defensible match. No specific Hyderabadi bread pudding entry exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for Double Ka Meetha or its closest analogue in the clinical terminology system.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of Double Ka Meetha is approximately 150 g, equivalent to two to three pieces of fried bread soaked in rabri, as typically served at Hyderabadi weddings or festive occasions.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods
nutrient_source.organization: National Institute of Nutrition (NIN) / ICMR, India
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Double Ka Meetha exists in the NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods database. Values are estimated by component analysis: deep-fried white bread (maida), ghee, sugar syrup, and rabri (thickened sweetened milk), cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for white bread, ghee, sugar, and whole milk. Saturated fat estimated from ghee content (~60% of total fat). Cholesterol estimated from ghee and full-cream milk contribution. Monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fat set to -1 as detailed fatty acid breakdown is not available in either NIN or USDA for this composite preparation.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — white bread and sugar-rich milk-based dessert analogues
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Double Ka Meetha. GI of 75 is estimated by analogy with white bread (GI ~71–75, University of Sydney GI Database) and sugar-rich milk-based Indian desserts. The high sugar content (38 g/100 g) and refined white bread base support a high GI classification. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (75 × 72.0) / 100 = 54.0, where carbohydrate per 150 g serving = 48.0 × 1.5 = 72.0 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Moderate sodium content (120 mg/100 g) and high saturated fat from ghee and full-cream milk
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit Double Ka Meetha to an occasional small portion (≤75 g) due to its saturated fat load from ghee and full-cream milk, which may adversely affect vascular tone and lipid profiles.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index (~75) and very high sugar content (38 g/100 g) from refined white bread and added sugar syrup
type2_diabetes.guidance: Double Ka Meetha is not recommended for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its high GI (~75) and very high sugar content (38 g/100 g), which cause rapid postprandial blood glucose elevation; if consumed, portion must be strictly limited to ≤50 g and paired with a low-GI meal.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High saturated fat (~13.5 g/100 g) from ghee and full-cream milk, combined with high caloric density (415 kcal/100 g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid or strictly limit Double Ka Meetha, as its high saturated fat content from ghee (~13.5 g/100 g) and caloric density (415 kcal/100 g) may raise LDL cholesterol and contribute to atherogenic risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Pro-inflammatory combination of high sugar, refined flour, and deep-fried preparation
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Deep-fried refined-flour preparations with high sugar content such as Double Ka Meetha may exacerbate systemic inflammation in individuals with rheumatoid arthritis and should be consumed rarely or avoided.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Double Ka Meetha is a rich, indulgent bread pudding originating from Hyderabad, Telangana, India, and is a hallmark dessert of Hyderabadi and Muslim Indian cuisine. The name derives from 'Double Roti', the local Urdu term for bread, which doubles in size during baking. To prepare this dessert, thick slices of white bread made from refined wheat flour (maida) are deep-fried in ghee until golden and crisp. The fried slices are then soaked in a fragrant sugar syrup infused with saffron and green cardamom, and finally slow-cooked in rabri — a thick, sweetened, reduced full-cream milk — until the bread absorbs the liquid and becomes soft, custardy, and pudding-like. The dish is traditionally garnished lavishly with fried cashews, almonds, and pistachios, creating a contrast of crispy nuts against the soft, creamy bread. Nutritionally, Double Ka Meetha is calorie-dense at approximately 415 kcal per 100 g, with high fat (22.5 g, predominantly saturated from ghee and full-cream milk at ~13.5 g/100 g), very high sugar (38 g/100 g), and moderate protein (5.2 g/100 g). Its glycemic index is estimated at approximately 75 (high), making it unsuitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes or those managing blood glucose. The dish is a staple at Hyderabadi wedding feasts and Eid-ul-Fitr celebrations, and is best consumed as an occasional festive treat in small portions. It is vegetarian and halal but contains gluten, dairy, and tree nuts.
common_user_queries: How is Double Ka Meetha made?

Can I eat Double Ka Meetha if I have high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Double Ka Meetha exists in the NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods or USDA FoodData Central databases. Nutrient values are estimated by component analysis of the known ingredients (white bread, ghee, sugar, full-cream milk, nuts) using NIN and USDA SR Legacy data. GI is estimated by analogy with white bread and sugar-rich milk-based desserts from the University of Sydney GI Database. Saturated fat and cholesterol are estimated from ghee and full-cream milk proportions. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown and moisture data for this composite deep-fried bread pudding preparation are not available in the NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods database or USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy as of 2024. 2. saturated_fat_g (13.5 g/100 g) and cholesterol_mg (55 mg/100 g) are estimated from the known ghee and full-cream milk content of the recipe, cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for ghee (NDB 04042) and whole milk. 3. GI of 75 is an analogous estimate based on white bread GI values from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for Double Ka Meetha has been published. Glycemic load per 150 g serving = 54.0. 4. The input record listed four identical duplicate portion entries; deduplicated to one standard serving of 150 g. 5. The input food_group was listed as 'Snacks'; reclassified to 'Desserts' as Double Ka Meetha is a traditional Indian sweet/dessert preparation, not a savoury snack. 6. FoodOn match FOODON_03315091 ('bread pudding food product') and FoodEx2 code A0CKP ('Bread pudding') are close but not exact matches; no specific Hyderabadi bread pudding code exists in either ontology system. 7. This dish is not a Singaporean dish; multilingual naming requirements for Chinese, Malay, and Tamil do not apply. Hindi, Telugu, and Urdu names are provided as the dish originates from Hyderabad, India.
